root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Organization Administration Guide
root.summary: Front matter and full introductory section for the Cúram 8.1.2 Organization Administration Guide. It identifies the guide’s purpose, audience, and prerequisites, then previews chapters on organization structure administration, managing/cloning structures, user administration, reporting hierarchy, and general organization administration. Core topics include the root organization unit, units, positions, user position assignment, navigation and administration security, user accounts/skills/jobs/working patterns, organizational reporting, locations, resources, web addresses, financial administration, calendars, and announcements with translation support. The conclusion summarizes key concepts and links to related Cúram and technical references. Also includes notices/legal terms, privacy policy, trademarks, copyright, and edition information for Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note directing the reader to review the Notices section before using the information or the supported product. Includes Merative US L.P. copyright years 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Covers the edition and the start of the Cúram Organization Administration Guide. Introduces the guide’s purpose, audience, prerequisites, and chapter map, then explains organization structure administration: root unit, organization units, positions, user assignments, list/tree navigation, and admin security. Describes SID-based unit security and sensitivity security for client/case data. Also begins managing the organization structure, including creation, activation status, and translating organization structure/unit names.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section covers organization administration on Cúram, including translating organization unit and job names, cloning/superseding/canceling organization structures, and user administration. It explains user account maintenance (delete, close/reopen, disable), user skills, jobs, positions, and working patterns. It also introduces organization reporting setup, lead/primary lead positions, reporting options, and how restructuring affects supervisor relationships. Finally, it outlines general organization administration from the home page: summaries, locations, resources, web addresses, and financial administration basics.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-30
children.summary: Covers organization administration concepts for finance and scheduling: bank branches/accounts, currency exchange rates, financial processing calendars and payment exclusion/prepayment rules. Also describes organization calendars for activities and recurring events, location holidays, working patterns, and application-wide announcements with translation support. The section concludes with a guide summary and lists related and technical reference documents.

Intro to the Cúram Organization Administration Guide (8.1–8.1.2) and overview of organization administration. Explains purpose, audience, prerequisites, and chapter map, then covers organization structure basics (root unit, units, positions, user assignments, navigation, admin security), structure management, user administration, reporting setup, and general administration. Also includes financial administration, calendars, working patterns, announcements with translation, and a concluding summary with related/technical references.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section provides legal notices for the document: sample data is fictitious, sample programs may be copied and modified under the Merative Client Agreement, but are offered “as is” without warranty or liability. It also points to Merative’s privacy policy URL and lists trademarks owned by Merative and other companies, plus the copyright notice.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists legal notices for sample data and programs, including fictitious-name disclaimer, copyright/license terms for sample code use, warranty/liability disclaimer, privacy policy link, and trademark ownership statements. Notes Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and other marks, plus © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication. Covers personal and enterprise reproduction permissions, required preservation of proprietary notices, and restrictions on distribution and derivative works. States no additional rights are granted, Merative may withdraw permission, and export laws apply. Includes an as-is/no-warranty disclaimer, patent notice, third-party product and website disclaimers, and copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Organization Administration Guide. Lists chapters and subsections covering organization structure administration, managing and cloning organization structures, user administration, reporting administration, and general organization administration topics such as locations, resources, calendars, working patterns, and announcements. Also includes conclusion, technical information, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation entry rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Cúram Organization Administration Guide
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: Overview of the Cúram Organization Administration Guide. It explains organization administration in Cúram Platform, focusing on managing organization users, financial administration, organization structure, and reporting hierarchy. It highlights that the organization structure represents users and functional areas and supports partitioning administration across hierarchy lines. The section also identifies the intended audience as business analysts with strong business knowledge but limited technical knowledge, and notes prerequisite familiarity with Social Enterprise Management and basic administrative tasks.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business analysts in the organization, with strong knowledge of business requirements but only basic application knowledge. The guide assumes low technical expertise, though some system administration terms may appear. It is relevant if you need to understand organization administration in Cúram, including user management, organization financial administration, organization structure, and reporting hierarchy, as well as prerequisites for managing social enterprise organization tasks.

children.title: Prerequisites
children.summary: Assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management basics and common organization administration tasks, especially managing users, organization administration, and reporting hierarchy. This section is a prerequisite note for the Cúram Organization Administration Guide, which covers organization structure, financial administration, user administration, and reporting hierarchy.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This section lists the guide’s main chapters and their purposes: organization structure administration, managing the organization structure, user administration, reporting administration, general organization administration, and the conclusion. It then introduces Organization Structure Administration, explaining the root organization unit, nested organization units, positions, user position assignments, and how the structure is navigated. It emphasizes that the organization chart is position-based, positions can be vacant or filled by users, and changing a user’s position does not change the overall structure.

Introduces the Cúram Organization Administration Guide and its scope: administering organization users, financial administration, organization structure, and reporting hierarchy. It explains that the organization structure represents users and functional areas and supports distributing administration across hierarchy lines. The guide is aimed at business analysts with strong business knowledge but limited technical background. It assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management basics and common organization administration tasks. The section also points readers to the guide’s chapter list.

children.title: 1.2 Organization Structure Administration
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Organization Structure Administration section of the Cúram guide. It explains the organization structure model: the root unit, organization units, positions, and user position assignments. It notes that units can contain other units and one or more positions, positions may be vacant or filled by registered users, and the structure represents roles rather than specific users. It also previews related guide chapters on structure administration, user administration, reporting, general administration, and the conclusion.

children.title: Organization Root Unit
children.summary: Introduces the Organization Structure Administration section and the organization chart model in Cúram. Explains that the root organization unit is the top-level unit containing all other organization units and positions. Notes that organization units can contain positions, positions may be filled or vacant, and users are assigned through position changes rather than altering the structure itself. Useful for understanding the root unit’s role and the basics of organization structure navigation and administration.

children.title: Organization Units, Positions, and User Position Assignment
children.summary: Explains how organization units contain positions, how positions are assigned to registered users, and how reporting relationships are derived from position assignments. Notes that every unit can have a default location, a position without a location inherits the unit’s default, and unassigned users are not represented in the organization structure or eligible for job-based work allocation. Also introduces organization structure navigation: list view vs. tree view, the available tabs, and how users browse or drill into units, positions, and users.

children.title: Navigating the Organization Structure
children.summary: Explains how to navigate the organization structure using two views: list view and tree view. The list view shows grouped tabs for organization units, positions, and users with expandable details; the tree view shows the same elements hierarchically for drilling down and viewing component details. Notes that organization structure tabs include Home, Tree View, Organization Units, Positions, and Users, and that pages provide action menus. Also mentions hierarchy rules: units can contain positions, positions can have multiple users, and users without a position are not represented.

children.title: Administration Security
Sensitivity Security
children.summary: Explains sensitivity security for protecting high-profile or risky data by assigning sensitivity levels to users, participants, notes, case notes, and service-plan items. Levels range from 1 to 5, with 5 highest; default is 1. Users can view or modify data only when their sensitivity level is equal to or higher than the data’s level. Also notes that when a case has multiple members, access decisions must consider the sensitivity levels of all involved members, not just one client.

This section explains administration security for organization units and sensitivity security for restricted data. It describes Read, Maintain, and Create Unit SIDs for organization units, including precedence rules and examples of access to parent and child units. It also covers sensitivity security levels 1–5 for users, participants, notes, case notes, and service-plan items, where access is allowed only when the user’s level is at least the data’s level. For cases with multiple members, all members’ sensitivity levels must be considered.

Overview of Cúram organization structure administration: the root unit, organization units, positions, and user position assignments. Explains that the structure is position-based, with units containing subunits and positions that can be filled or vacant, and that user changes affect assignments, not the structure. Covers navigation in list and tree views, including tabs and drill-down behavior. Also summarizes administration security for unit SIDs (Read, Maintain, Create Unit) and sensitivity security levels 1–5 for restricted data and case access.

children.title: 1.3 Managing the Organization Structure
Introduction
children.summary: Explains case access rules when multiple case members have different sensitivity levels: access is based on the highest sensitivity among case participants, the primary client’s sensitivity is central, and users may be denied, masked, or allowed view-only access depending on their level. Includes two examples illustrating access and modification restrictions. Also introduces managing the organization structure, which covers creating, activating, superseding, canceling, and cloning an organization structure, and notes possible statuses: active, in edit, superseded, and canceled.

children.title: Creating an Organization Structure
Translating Organization Structure and Organization Unit Names
children.summary: This section explains how to translate an organization structure name in Cúram for multilingual applications. It notes that organization structures can be translated and displayed at runtime based on the user’s locale, or edited in the active single non-English language. It provides step-by-step instructions for administrators to add or modify a translation via Administration Workspace > My Organization > Organization Structures, using the Translate Text icon. It also mentions that the translated name appears in caseworker views and points to related documentation.

Explains that an organization structure must be created before organization units, appears in tree or non-tree form, and starts in “in edit” status until activated. The first component is the root organization unit, with child units and positions added beneath it. Also introduces translation of organization structure and unit names for multilingual deployments, including how runtime language is chosen by user locale and how to add or edit a structure-name translation via Administration Workspace > My Organization > Organization Structures.

children.title: Superseding and Canceling the Organization Structure
children.summary: Explains how organization structures can be superseded, canceled, or cloned. An active organization structure becomes superseded when replaced during activation by an in-edit or superseded structure. Only structures with status in edit or superseded can be deleted/canceled; in-edit means not in use, and superseded means no longer in use. Also notes that active organization structures and their components can be cloned, creating new relationships among positions, organization units, and users for the cloned structure.

children.title: Cloning the Organization Structure
children.summary: This section explains that an active organization structure can be cloned along with all its components. Cloning creates a new relationship for the cloned structure, linking its Positions, Organization Units, and Users to those from the active organization structure. It also notes related lifecycle states for organization structures, including superseded and canceled statuses.

Covers managing organization structures: creating one before defining units, starting in “in edit” status, activating it, and building it from a root organization unit with child units and positions. Describes multilingual translation of organization structure and organization unit names via Administration Workspace, with runtime language based on user locale. Also explains lifecycle actions: active structures can be superseded on activation of another structure, in-edit or superseded structures can be canceled, and active structures can be cloned with their positions, units, and users.

children.title: 1.4 User Administration
Introduction
children.summary: This introduction explains Cúram organization administration for managing users and their account data. It covers user registration, stored details such as positions, jobs, skills, contact information, and security settings. It highlights user account maintenance, including login credentials, access periods, enabled status, security roles, location-based security, and sensitivity access levels. It also notes that administrators can search for closed and deleted users, and that security can be tailored per individual user rather than only by position or role.

children.title: Maintaining User Accounts
Deleting User Accounts
children.summary: Explains user account lifecycle actions in organization administration. Covers deleting accounts (allowed for erroneous accounts; also deletes held positions, but not if the user has case roles or open tasks), closing/reopening accounts (sets an end date and redirects tasks/notifications, but not if the user has reports or open tasks), and disabling accounts (prevents login until re-enabled). Also notes that user summary pages are available only in the tree view, and introduces user skills, jobs, positions, and matching work to users by skills and reporting structure.

children.title: Closing and Reopening User Accounts
children.summary: This section explains user account lifecycle actions: deleting accounts (with limits if the user holds roles on stored cases or open tasks), closing and reopening accounts, and disabling accounts. Closing records an end date and redirects tasks/notifications to another user; it cannot occur if the user still has reports or open tasks. It also introduces task redirection for temporary absence and notes that user summary pages are available in the tree organization view.

children.title: Disabling User Accounts
children.summary: This section explains user account lifecycle actions in organization administration. It distinguishes deleting accounts, closing/reopening accounts, and disabling accounts. Deleted accounts remove the user’s positions and cannot be deleted if they still hold case roles or have open tasks. Closed accounts record an end date and redirect tasks/notifications, but cannot be closed if they manage others or still have open tasks. Disabled accounts prevent login until re-enabled, useful for accounts created in advance.

children.title: Summary
children.summary: This section explains user account administration and organization user summaries. It covers deleting accounts, noting that deletion also removes held positions and is blocked if the user has open tasks or case roles; closing accounts with end dates, task redirection, and restrictions; and disabling accounts so users cannot log in. It also introduces the user summary page in the tree organization view, and discusses matching users to jobs and work using skills, competencies, reporting hierarchy, and job templates.

Covers user account administration for organization users. Explains what is stored in a user account: contact details, login credentials, access periods, enabled/disabled status, security roles, location-based security, and sensitivity access level. Notes that administrators can search for closed and deleted users, and that closed users can also appear in regular search. Also introduces user account maintenance as part of matching user skills with jobs/positions and supporting user supervision and working patterns.

children.title: User Skills, Jobs and Positions
Skills
children.summary: Explains how user skills are managed in organization administration. Members can have graded skills and competencies (for example, counseling, computing, language), supported by competency models and job templates. The system maintains a database of user skills and abilities linked to each user profile so work can be assigned to the most appropriate user. This section sits within the broader topic of user skills, jobs, and positions and connects skills to work allocation and organizational staffing.

children.title: Jobs
Translating Job Names
children.summary: Explains how to translate job names in Cúram for multilingual applications. An administrator navigates to Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization, opens the Jobs tab, and uses Translate Text to add or edit translations for a job title. The section notes that the correct language is chosen at runtime from the user’s locale, and that in a single-language non-English setup, the job name can be edited directly in that language.

This section defines jobs as users’ functional roles in Cúram (for example, social worker or claims analyst) and explains that jobs can be mapped to users through position assignment to support work allocation. It also covers translating job names for multilingual applications: administrators can add or edit translation text from Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization > Jobs, and the runtime language is chosen from the user’s locale. In single-language non-English setups, the job name can be edited directly.

children.title: Positions
Translating Position Names
children.summary: Explains how to translate position names in Cúram organization administration. Positions are placeholders in the organization structure that define reporting relationships, can contain jobs, and may have locations. If multiple languages are supported, administrators can add or modify translation text for position names so users see the correct language at runtime; if only one non-English language is supported, the position name can be edited directly. Includes navigation steps to the Positions list under Organization Structures.

Explains what positions are in Cúram organization administration: placeholders in the org structure that define reporting relationships, can contain multiple jobs, and may have assigned locations affecting access when location-based security is used. Also notes that users’ positions determine supervisors and reporting hierarchy. Includes instructions for adding or editing translation text for position names so they display in the user’s locale at runtime, with navigation to the Positions list under Organization Structures.

Explains how Cúram links user skills, jobs, and positions for work allocation and reporting. Skills are graded competencies stored on user profiles, supported by competency models and job templates, enabling assignment based on expertise and language skills. Jobs are users’ functional roles and can be mapped to users through position assignment; job names can be translated by locale. Positions define reporting hierarchy, can contain multiple jobs, may include locations for location-based security, and also support translated position names.

children.title: User Supervision
children.summary: Explains user supervision in Cúram: every user reports to at least one supervisor, and supervisors may have a special Supervisor Workspace to manage tasks and cases. It lists who they can directly manage: users in units where they hold a lead role, users in child units of those units, and users in positions reporting directly to their position. Also notes related documentation for the Supervisor Workspace Guide.

children.title: User Working Patterns
children.summary: This section explains how user working patterns are defined and managed. It says organization-level working patterns act as defaults for system users, but individual user patterns override them. Each pattern covers a 14-day period starting Monday and ending the second Sunday, can repeat indefinitely if no end date is set, and multiple patterns may exist as long as their dates do not overlap.

Cúram user administration covers managing organization users and their accounts, including contact details, login credentials, access periods, enabled/disabled status, security roles, location-based security, and sensitivity levels. It explains deleting, closing, reopening, and disabling accounts, plus user summary pages. The section also links users to skills, jobs, and positions for work allocation and reporting, including translated names. It describes supervision relationships and supervisor task management, and defines organization and individual user working patterns over repeating 14-day periods.

children.title: 1.5 Organization Reporting Administration
Introduction
children.summary: Explains organization reporting administration: how the chain of command is established from organization structure and user position assignments. Covers setting up reporting by assigning users to lead positions, the role of lead positions in determining supervisors, and the primary lead position required at the root organization unit. Also describes how reporting is inherited when no lead exists in a unit or its parents, and how users typically report to the lead position within the same organization unit.

children.title: Setting Up Organization Reporting
Lead Positions and the Primary Lead Position
children.summary: Explains organization reporting and how users are assigned supervisors through lead positions. Defines lead positions as supervisory roles over reporting positions, and introduces the primary lead position assigned to the root organization unit to ensure every position has a reporting path. Describes fallback behavior when a unit has no lead position: reporting follows the organization structure up to a parent lead position, or to the primary lead position if none exist. Also notes that user-to-position assignments determine reporting relationships.

children.title: Reporting Positions
children.summary: Explains how organization reporting defines the chain of command through position assignments. Users assigned to lead positions supervise users in reporting positions within the same organization unit. Describes the primary lead position on the root unit, how reporting falls back to parent units or the primary lead when no lead exists, and notes that user-to-supervisor relationships depend on position assignments. Useful for understanding how supervisor relationships are established and affected by organization structure changes.

Explains how to set up organization reporting, which defines the chain of command in an organization. Users assigned to lead positions supervise users in reporting positions within the same unit. A primary lead position must exist on the root organization unit to ensure every position has a reporting path. If a unit has no lead position, reporting falls back through parent units to the primary lead position. User-supervisor relationships depend on position assignments and can change when the organization structure changes.

children.title: Relationship between Users and their Supervisors
User in Supervisor Role Leaves Organization
children.summary: If a supervisor leaves, affected users should temporarily report to the next most senior supervisor until a new supervisor is assigned. The organization can also adjust reporting without changing the structure, such as redirecting users in the same organization unit to another position or editing a position to report to a user in another unit. This section explains how supervisor departures impact reporting hierarchies and how reporting relationships can be adapted.

children.title: A Group of Users Reporting to Different Supervisors
children.summary: Explains that user-supervisor reporting is tied to organization structure, but can be reassigned flexibly when supervisors leave or structures change. Covers cases where users in an organization unit report to lead positions in the same unit or to parent/grandparent units if no lead exists. Also notes configurable reporting options for positions: lead positions, same-unit positions, parent-unit positions, or any position in the organization. Introduces the more complex case of a group of users reporting to different supervisors, such as cross-functional teams.

children.title: Impact of Restructuring Organization Positions
children.summary: Explains how restructuring organization positions affects reporting relationships: positions may be changed or become redundant, but still define supervisor links, so users must be reassigned carefully to new positions and reporting positions. Notes that organizations should focus on positions, not just people, and that users should be placed based on role and reporting needs. Also covers handling users with no supervisors by assigning them to the root unit’s lead position, and introduces general organization administration and the organization home page.

children.title: Handling Users with No Supervisors
children.summary: Explains how to handle users without supervisors: assign them to the lead position of the root organization unit, such as a CEO who reports to a board rather than an individual supervisor. This section sits in the context of managing reporting hierarchies and warns that restructuring positions can disrupt user-supervisor relationships, so users and supervisors may need reassignment when positions change.

Explains how user-to-supervisor reporting depends on organization structure and how it changes when supervisors leave or positions are restructured. Covers fallback reporting to the next senior supervisor, redirecting users to another position in the same unit or another unit, and configurable reporting targets (lead, same-unit, parent-unit, or any position). Also introduces more complex scenarios such as users in cross-functional teams reporting to different supervisors.

Explains organization reporting as the chain of command based on position assignments. Covers assigning users to lead positions, the primary lead position required at the root unit, and fallback reporting through parent units when a unit has no lead or assigned user. Describes how users typically report within the same unit, but can also be configured to report to lead, same-unit, parent-unit, or any position. Also covers how supervisor departures or structural changes can redirect reporting relationships, including cross-functional teams.

children.title: 1.6 General Organization Administration
Organization and Location Summary
children.summary: This section explains the organization home page summary shown to administrators: active organization and location structure, including counts of units, positions, users, and maintained locations. It also notes that the home page is the main entry point for administering users, jobs, organization structures, location structures, activity calendars, and working patterns, while finance information is accessed separately from the administration home page.

children.title: The Organization Home Page
children.summary: This section explains the organization home page as the central entry point for administering organization details. It lists what can be managed there: system users, jobs, organization structures, location structures, activity calendars, and employee working patterns. It also notes the home page shows a summary of the active organization and location structure, including counts of units, positions, users, and locations. Finance information is the main exception and is accessed from the administration home page.

children.title: Locations
children.summary: This section explains organization locations and related administration. It defines locations as users’ workplaces in a hierarchical structure with one root location, and notes that locations can have holidays, working patterns, resources, and daily schedules. It also covers public offices, shared addresses, and location security controlling access to case and client data. Briefly, it mentions resources assigned to locations and organization units, web addresses managed from the organization home page, and the start of financial administration topics.

children.title: Resources
children.summary: Resources are organization assets such as tools, equipment, and office spaces used to support work—for example, printers or conference rooms. They can be assigned to organization locations and organization units. The surrounding page also notes that locations may have security controls, but this section itself focuses on defining resources and their assignment within the organization structure.

children.title: Organization Web Addresses
children.summary: This section explains that web addresses for an organization or organization unit can be managed from the organization home page. Users can create, modify, and delete these web address records there.

children.title: Organization Financial Administration
Banks and Bank Branches
children.summary: This section introduces organization financial administration and specifically the Banks and Bank Branches topic. It defines banks as financial institutions used to keep, lend, invest, or exchange money, and bank branches as the facilities serving specific bank locations. It appears alongside related financial administration content such as currency exchange rates, organization bank accounts, and financial processing calendars.

children.title: Currency Exchange Rates
children.summary: Explains currency exchange rates in financial administration. An organization has a base currency, and exchange rates stored in the application are used to support payments and receipts in other currencies, always expressed relative to the base currency. Example given: a Euro-based organization paying a participant in Sterling using a Euro/GBP rate. The section also transitions into financial processing calendars and organization calendars.

children.title: Financial Processing Calendar
children.summary: Explains financial processing calendars by delivery method (cash, check, EFT, voucher, invoice) and how they track dates when payments cannot be made. Defines payment exclusion dates, notes that due payments on those dates should be moved to a prior batch run, and gives the Dec. 25 cash holiday example. Also covers prepayment requirements, where processing occurs on the nearest earlier processing date before the exclusion date.

Organization Financial Administration covers maintaining the organization’s financial setup: banks and bank branches, currency exchange rates, organization bank accounts, and the financial processing calendar. It explains that banks are institutions where money is kept, lent, invested, or exchanged, and bank branches are the facilities tied to specific bank locations. This node is useful if you need configuration details for organizational banking, payment currencies, or delivery-method-specific processing dates.

children.title: Organization Calendars
Scheduling Organization Activities
children.summary: Explains organization calendar scheduling: administrators can record once-off and recurring organization activities. These activities are not linked to a concern or case, and invitees cannot be selected. Useful for understanding basic organization-level calendar functions rather than case management or participant scheduling.

children.title: Scheduling a Recurring Organization Activity
children.summary: Explains recurring organization activities in Cúram: users can set an activity to repeat with a chosen frequency, assign it to an occurrence or the full series, and edit updates accordingly. The section also covers related scheduling features on the same page: location holidays from location calendars, organization working patterns as default 14-day schedules for users, and application-wide announcements that can be timed and translated for display on home pages.

children.title: Scheduling Location Holidays
children.summary: This section explains scheduling location holidays in Cúram. Holidays are set from a location calendar and appear in calendars for all users at that location; they may also apply to sublocations. Example: a public holiday can be recorded for a main office and inherited by its sublocations, showing on all affected users’ calendars.

Overview of organization calendar functions. Covers scheduling once-off and recurring organization activities by administrators; these activities are not linked to concerns or cases and do not support invitee selection. Also introduces related calendar features such as recurring organization activities, location holidays from location calendars, default organization working patterns, and timed/translatable application-wide announcements.

children.title: Organization Working Patterns
children.summary: This section explains organization working patterns as default schedules for all system users, overridable by individual user patterns. It notes that organization and user working-pattern functions are the same, each pattern spans a 14-day period from Monday to the second Sunday, uses standard hours such as 9 AM–5 PM, and multiple patterns are allowed as long as their dates do not overlap.

children.title: Announcements
Translating Announcement Text and Comments
children.summary: Explains how administrators translate announcement text and comments in Cúram. Covers navigating to Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization, opening Announcements, using Translate Text, then adding a new translation or editing an existing one for a selected language. Notes that the application displays announcement text based on the user’s locale at runtime, and that if only one non-English language is supported, the announcement can be edited directly in that language.

This section explains application-wide announcements on the home page and how they can be translated for multilingual Cúram deployments. It covers the purpose of announcements, their runtime display based on the user’s locale, and the administrator workflow for translating announcement text and comments via Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization > Announcements. It also notes that administrators can add or edit translations, and that if only one non-English language is supported, the announcement can be edited directly in that language.

Covers general organization administration from the organization home page: the summary of active organization/location structure and counts, and access to administer users, jobs, organization and location structures, activity calendars, working patterns, and web addresses. Explains location administration basics (hierarchy, holidays, security, resources) and finance setup (banks, branches, accounts, exchange rates, financial processing calendar). Also describes organization calendars for once-off/recurring activities, location holidays, default working patterns, and application-wide announcements with multilingual translation.

children.title: 1.7 Conclusion
Summary
children.summary: This section summarizes the guide’s main concepts: organizational maintenance of facilities, activities, and financial data; skills and competencies linked to users for work assignment; jobs and positions and how users map to them; supervisor relationships and reporting via position-based organizational structure; access control for organization administration; and calendar administration, including organization, location holiday, and financial payment dates. It also points to related guides for participant functions and case processing.

children.title: Additional information
children.summary: This section points readers to related documentation for further details. It mainly references the Cúram Participant Guide, which covers participant functions, and the Integrated Case Management Guide, which covers case processing. It follows a summary of the guide’s main concepts, including organization structure, users, jobs and positions, supervisors, calendars, and financial/payment scheduling.

children.title: Technical Information
children.summary: This section lists technical reference documents related to the Cúram platform, including the Rules Editor, Eligibility and Entitlement Engine, Batch Processing, and Operations guides. It also includes legal and administrative notices: publication permissions, copyright and export restrictions, “as-is” warranty disclaimer, sample code license terms, privacy policy link, and trademark notices. Open this node if you need related technical document pointers or the legal/use terms for the guide.

Conclusion section with links to the guide’s summary and related documentation. It recaps core organization administration topics: facilities, activities, financial data, skills/competencies for work assignment, jobs and positions, supervisor/reporting relationships, security for org administration, and organization/location/financial calendars and payment dates. It also points to related guides such as Participant, Integrated Case Management, Evidence, Financials, Deductions, Communications, Workflow, Verification Engine, and Location Administration, plus technical references for Rules Editor, Eligibility/Entitlement Engine, Batch Processing, and Operations. Includes notices, licensing, privacy, and trademarks.

Overview of Cúram organization administration: it explains the guide’s purpose, audience, and chapters, then covers organization structure administration (root unit, units, positions, user assignments, list/tree navigation, SIDs, sensitivity security). It also summarizes managing structures, user administration, reporting hierarchy, and general administration such as locations, finance setup, calendars, working patterns, and announcements. The conclusion points to related Cúram guides and technical references, followed by notices, privacy, and trademarks.
